_comment: Human-readable changelog for the dashboard's About tab -- written for people, not commit archaeologists. The dashboard fetches this from GitHub at page load (baked copy as fallback), so add an entry here with each release and every install sees it. Newest first. Dates are YYYY-MM-DD.

entries.title: Graph header: steadier, and properly purple
notes: The Graph's rate arrows are now slim stroke-style arrows (stem and chevron) instead of solid triangles, matching the dashboard preview's look.
Upload purple is now the exact shade from the reference design, on both the device and the preview.

The rate readouts sit in fixed slots -- arrows, text, and baseline never shift as the numbers change width, so the header stops dancing during a busy transfer. One font size fits everything up to a two-way gigabit, verified against the actual glyph tables.

entries.title: ZimaOS Graph: watch your traffic roll by
notes: New network layout: a rolling graph of the last few minutes of traffic, download in teal and upload in purple, each a soft filled area with a bright line. Newest data on the right, one sample per second.

No axes on purpose -- the graph continuously rescales itself to the tallest thing on screen, so a quiet trickle and a full-speed download both draw an interesting picture. The live rates sit up top behind little arrows.
The rate readouts up top use compact units (Kb / Mb / Gb) and step down a font size when both numbers get long, so a two-way gigabit moment never runs off the edge.
History records in the background regardless of which layout is showing, so the moment you switch to the Graph it already has a story to tell.

entries.title: Dots fit right, RAM reads right, and the page dots retire
notes: The Dots grid no longer crowds the right edge -- its spacing is now computed from the actual panel width (a height-based scaling bug pushed it off the edge on the 240x280 screen), and the dots slimmed down a touch for comfortable margins on every panel.
The RAM dial now shows total installed memory -- the same number your OS reports (15.5 GB on a 16 GB stick, after the system's own reservations).
On the network card, 'Download' politely steps aside as 'Down..' whenever the rate text would crowd it -- measured against the actual rendered widths, not a guess.
The layouts got proper names: ZimaOS Mist, ZimaOS Dial, Zima App Bars, and ZimaOS Dots. Your saved settings carry over unchanged.
The little page-indicator dots at the bottom of the screen are gone -- swiping needs no map, and every layout gets the breathing room back.

entries.title: Network speaks Mbps, RAM shows what's free, and storage gets Dots
notes: The Bars network layout now shows rates in megabits -- the same language your speedtest uses. A 950 Mbps test previously displayed as '113.2MB' (megaBYTES per second, the identical speed in confusing clothing); now it just says 950 Mbps. The bars were already scaled to a full gigabit link and stay that way.
The RAM dial's bottom number now shows available memory instead of memory in use, as intended.

New 'Dots' layout for the MMC and NAS pages: a 12x4 grid of dots that fills column by column as the volume fills up, with the available space headlined below. The dots glow green until 75% used, amber to 95%, and red beyond -- a fuel gauge you can read from across the room.
The Dots card headlines your volume: the system disk shows as ZimaOS-HD (override with TINYSCREEN_MMC_LABEL if yours differs), and the NAS card names your attached drive.

entries.title: Bigger dial text, and a Bars layout for the network
notes: The dial's label and stats line grew to a comfortable size, and the big percentage now sits at exactly 1.5x the rows below it (two new font sizes baked into the firmware make this possible).

The network page gets an alternate 'Bars' layout: a widget-style card showing which interface the traffic is flowing through, with Upload and Download rates and slim blue activity bars that fill against a gigabit link.
The collector now reports the name of your busiest network interface (usually just eth0 on a ZimaBlade) so the card can headline it.
Pick it in the Layouts tab, same as Mist and Dial.

entries.title: Dial fit and finish
notes: The dial's ring is meatier on the device (it was rendering skinnier than intended), while the dashboard preview slimmed down a touch -- the two now carry the same visual weight.
The big percentage is now truly centered on the dial; the little % sign rides along on the right without dragging the number off-axis.

entries.title: New Dial layout for CPU and RAM
notes: CPU and RAM each get an alternate 'Dial' layout: a speedometer-style ring with its opening at the bottom, a big percentage in the middle, and the useful numbers underneath -- power draw and temperature for the CPU, gigabytes in use for RAM.
The dial's ring color follows the load: calm green up to 60%, warming to amber by 80%, and red past 95%. A box under strain announces itself from across the room.
If your hardware doesn't report CPU power draw, the dial quietly shows just the temperature instead of a bogus zero.
Pick layouts per page in the Layouts tab, same as the Mist layouts for temperature.

entries.title: The corona stretches its legs
notes: The Mist glow now reaches all the way out -- its furthest edges brush the top-left and bottom-right corners, matching the preview instead of stopping halfway. It also runs a touch brighter on the device, because the little panel swallows dark shades harder than a computer monitor does.

Particles now radiate from the corner in every direction -- straight up, straight right, and everything in between -- instead of marching single-file toward the middle. They still fade out about half a screen from home.
Fixed along the way: a faded particle sitting on the bright part of the glow could punch a dark hole in it. Particles now add their light on top of the glow instead of replacing it.

entries.title: Mist, take three -- and we have a logo
notes: The Mist glow is finally smooth. Turns out the little screen's colors are chunky enough that any ring-based gradient shows contour lines, so the glow is now drawn pixel by pixel with a touch of noise mixed in to hide the steps. No more banding.

The mist moved to the bottom-LEFT corner, where it always belonged, and the background speckle is gone entirely -- the animated layout's rising particles are the only mist particles now, and they fade out by about halfway up the screen.
The big temperature number is now roughly twice the size, in a new jumbo digits-only font. If your CPU somehow hits triple digits, it politely steps down a size instead of running off the screen.
TinyScreen has a logo! The hand-made wordmark now headlines the About tab, retiring the dashed placeholder that's been holding its seat.

entries.title: Smooth fonts and a thicker mist
notes: The screen finally types like the previews: blocky pixel text is gone, replaced by smooth proportional fonts (DejaVu Sans, rendered right on the device). Every page benefits.
The default gauges now genuinely match the dashboard previews -- title up top, rounded-cap progress arc, big clean percentage in the middle.
The Mist layout got a lot mistier: a proper glow rising from the corner, about three times the speckle, and twice the drifting particles in the animated variant.
Needs firmware v1.5.0 (included) -- update the app, then flash from the General tab.

entries.title: Layout styles arrive
notes: The layout dropdowns on the Layouts page are real now! Every page has the classic look, named 'TinyScreen (default)'.

CPU Temperature gets the first alternates: 'Mist' -- a widget-style card with a big temperature number and a colored glow rising from the corner -- and 'Mist (animated)', where little pixels drift out of the mist and fade away.
The number and the mist both shift from green through amber to red as your CPU warms up, and the dashboard previews show exactly the style you've picked.
Needs firmware v1.4.0 (included) -- update the app, then flash from the General tab.

entries.title: Sub-pages and small comforts
notes: The About page slimmed down to fit on one screen: the changelog shows just the latest release with a View more page behind it, and the credits became a tidy strip of names and faces with a Learn more page for the full write-ups.
View Logs works now -- the Debugging section opens a full page with the last firmware update's output and the app's raw status, right there when you need it.
Unplugging the screen no longer errors out the whole dashboard: everything stays browsable with a clear 'No device detected' warning.
Plus a batch of General-tab alignment fixes on desktop and mobile, and a reserved spot for the TinyScreen logo (coming soon to a repo near you).

entries.title: General tab, comfier
notes: The screen preview on the General tab is now front and center at a proper size, and device health shows as a tidy status pill -- including which ZimaBoard it's connected to.
On phones, the General tab breathes: the preview slides in under the device name, and every card's button gets its own row.

entries.title: A friendlier front page
notes: The General tab got a redesign: your device card now shows a live-style preview of the screen, uses the same naming as the setup wizard, and only offers a firmware update button when there's actually an update.
Checking for app updates is now a button, no page refresh needed -- and updating shows a proper confirmation window with the patch notes right there.

entries.title: Screen page upgrades
notes: Two new screen options: Rotation (mount your screen sideways or upside down and keep everything right-side up) and, on the 1.69-inch display, a square 1:1 aspect ratio mode. Both need firmware v1.3.0, included in this release.
Night Mode now handles daylight saving time on its own -- no more re-saving your schedule when the clocks change.
Brightness can now go all the way to off, the sliders look at home in the dark theme, and the Night Mode controls fit properly on phones.
Fixed the white flashes at the screen edges on iPhones, and locked scrolling to the page content so the toolbar stays put.

entries.title: Carousel fine-tuning
notes: Fixed the preview gauges looking slightly off-center (the colored arc wasn't sitting on its track).
When the carousel wraps to a second row on narrower windows, the thumbnails now stay the same size across rows.
'Temperature' is now 'CPU Temperature', to be clearer about what it's measuring.

entries.title: Layouts polish
notes: Fixed the save banner sticking around after saving on Safari, and made it fit comfortably on phone screens.
CPU, RAM, and Network pages are now called 'Utilization' (with tidy short names on the previews), the carousel thumbnails grew to fill the row, and the previews lost their little page-indicator dots.
On phones, each page's preview and layout picker now stack neatly in the center instead of spilling off the edge.

entries.title: Layouts, reimagined
notes: The Layouts page matches the original design vision: a visual carousel strip up top showing your pages in order (drag to rearrange, or use the little arrows), and each page in its own card below with a live-style preview and a spot for layout styles (coming soon).
Automatic Carousel is now a simple toggle, with the timing options appearing when it's on.
Saving got smarter: a banner slides down whenever you have unsaved changes and follows you between tabs, with Save and Discard right there. No more save buttons scattered around.

entries.title: Little touches
notes: The dashboard window is a bit shorter and better proportioned on desktop.
The navigation got icons -- and on phones it slims down to just the icons, so nothing scrolls sideways.

entries.title: A proper window
notes: The dashboard now lives in its own self-contained window -- sidebar and content each get their own panel, floating over the same ambient background as the setup wizard. It should feel right at home next to ZimaOS's own settings.
Works on small screens too: the window goes edge-to-edge and the navigation moves to the top.

entries.title: Wizard glow-up
notes: The setup wizard got friendlier: clearer language throughout, proper names for both screens, links to where you can buy them, and smooth transitions between steps.
Finishing setup now takes you to the dashboard (it used to drop you on an old settings page).
Retired three leftover pages from earlier in the project -- old links to them redirect to the right place.

entries.title: Reset Device
notes: The Reset button on the General tab now works: it erases the display's saved settings and walks you back through first-time setup. Handy if something gets into a weird state.
Comes with a confirm step, so a stray click can't wipe anything.
Needs firmware v1.2.0 (included) -- update the app, then flash the firmware from the General tab.

entries.title: Night Mode and Screensaver
notes: Night Mode: dim the screen (or turn it off entirely) on a daily schedule -- great for a display that lives in your bedroom or office.
Screensaver: on touch displays, show a drifting clock or blank the screen after a few idle minutes. Tap to wake.
This is the first firmware update since launch (v1.1.0) -- after updating the app, head to the General tab and hit Check for Update to flash it.

entries.title: The About page
notes: The About tab is now a real page: project info, credits for the open-source projects TinyScreen is built on, license details, and this changelog.
All of it is pulled straight from the GitHub repo, so it stays current without needing an app update.

entries.title: A tiny test balloon
notes: Added an About tab placeholder to the sidebar.
Honestly, this release mostly existed to prove the new one-click updater worked on real hardware. (It did.)

entries.title: One-click updates
notes: The Update App button now actually updates the app: one click (plus a confirm), about 30 seconds, done.
If anything goes wrong mid-update, it automatically rolls back to the version you were on and tells you what happened.
Versions now have friendly numbers like this one, instead of git commit codes.
Fixed the dashboard opening on the Layouts tab while the sidebar claimed you were on General.

entries.title: Update detection
notes: The dashboard now notices when a newer version of the app has been published and says so on the General tab.
